nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still blackwater streams, forest streams (igarapés), and flooded forest margins in the upper Rio Juruá drainage of the western Brazilian Amazon; leaf-litter zones and shaded marginal vegetation

decorPreferences: leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves, oak leaves) for tannins and refuge
caves and coconut shells for spawning sites
dense marginal planting (Cryptocoryne, Java fern, Anubias) for female refuge
dim or broken lighting (floating plants recommended)

diet.notes: In the wild, the Juruá Dwarf Cichlid feeds primarily on small invertebrates. In the aquarium it accepts a range of live and frozen micro-foods, including baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii), Cyclops, Daphnia, micro-worms, grindal worms, and bloodworms — the last of these should be offered sparingly rather than as a staple. Well-settled individuals often accept high-quality small-particle carnivore micro-pellets. A varied live and frozen diet is considered essential for conditioning breeding adults and for raising fry successfully.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras tolerating acidic water (Paracheirodon axelrodi, Hyphessobrycon species)
small Corydoras or Otocinclus in lower bioload setups
dither fish that share blackwater parameters
avoidWith: other Apistogramma species or conspecific males (territorial conflicts)
fast or boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
shrimp (will be preyed upon, especially small Neocaridina/Caridina)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo page for Apistogramma juruensis; available images may include wild-collected males showing typical pattern.
Wikimedia Commons category page; check for freely-licensed images of males and females.

summary: The Juruá Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma juruensis) is a specialist blackwater species from the Rio Juruá drainage of western Brazil, belonging to the agassizii species group. Multiple locality color forms are known. It demands very soft, acidic water and cave-spawning conditions, and is best suited to experienced Apistogramma keepers.
careNotes:
The Juruá Dwarf Cichlid (Apistogramma juruensis) is a member of the agassizii species group and originates exclusively from blackwater streams of the Rio Juruá system in Amazonas and Acre states, western Brazil. It is generally considered one of the more demanding Apistogramma species in the hobby. Water chemistry is critical: pH must be maintained in the acidic range (4.0–6.5) with very soft water (GH 0–5 °dH, KH 0–2 °dH). Using RO water remineralised to very low TDS (50–100 µS) and conditioned with tannin-releasing botanicals (catappa leaves, alder cones, driftwood) is standard practice. Chlorinated or hard tap water is unsuitable and is likely to cause chronic stress, immune suppression, and breeding failure. Multiple locality forms (colour variants) are documented from different tributaries within the Juruá drainage; locality provenance should be tracked and mixing of forms is generally avoided to preserve genetic integrity.

A species-typical harem group consists of one male and two to four females spread across a well-structured tank. Males are significantly larger than females and defend territories around cave sites; females hold tighter spawning territories inside caves. A minimum footprint of 80 cm is needed for a single pair; larger tanks (100–120 cm+) allow successful haremic groups. Each female requires at least one cave or coconut-shell spawning site, with visual barriers (dense planting, driftwood) between female territories to reduce inter-female aggression. Leaf litter is strongly recommended, both for the tannins it releases and as foraging substrate. Dim lighting reduces stress. Regular small partial water changes (10–20% twice weekly) with matched soft, acidic water maintain quality without parameter shock. Activated carbon in filtration should be avoided, as it strips the tannins that are important to the species' well-being.
breedingNotes: The Juruá Dwarf Cichlid is a cave-spawner in which females deposit eggs inside caves or crevices and provide intensive brood care while the male defends the outer territory perimeter. Males are haremic and may fertilise multiple females simultaneously in a large, well-structured setup. Breeding is typically triggered by stable, very soft, acidic blackwater conditions combined with a varied live and frozen diet. A water temperature of 26–28 °C and pH of 4.5–5.5 with TDS below 80 µS is associated with the best spawning success. Clutch size is typically 40–100 eggs. Females tend to become strongly defensive of caves during spawning and for several weeks after the fry hatch; the male should be removable if aggression becomes dangerous in smaller tanks. Fry are free-swimming after approximately 10–14 days post-hatching and initially accept baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii) and micro-worms. Soft, acidic water is considered essential for fry development, as hard or alkaline water tends to cause high fry mortality. Raising F1 specimens from wild-collected or locality-pure parents is recommended to preserve the integrity of individual locality forms.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Apistogramma juruensis
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided water parameters (pH 4.0–6.0, hardness 18–90 µS, temperature 24–29°C), adult size (males to 8 cm, females to 4.5 cm), tank requirements, diet, breeding, and locality-form notes. Confirmed agassizii-group placement and Rio Juruá origin.

additional.name: FishBase — Apistogramma juruensis
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), native range (Rio Juruá drainage, western Brazil), adult size (males to 7.7 cm TL), and temperature range. Confirmed freshwater obligate; no salinity tolerance data.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Apistogramma Care Guide
additional.notes: General Apistogramma genus care guide used to cross-check hobbyist-level husbandry guidance on tank setup, diet, water chemistry, and harem grouping strategies applicable to A. juruensis.

additional.notes: TFH Magazine and associated cichlid literature (Römer's Cichlid Atlas) used for locality form documentation, agassizii-group classification, and wild biotope characterisation of the Juruá blackwater drainage.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Dwarf Cichlid Blackwater Setups
additional.notes: Cross-checked advanced care designation, RO water and tannin requirements, and general guidance on keeping specialist Apistogramma species demanding strict blackwater conditions.

fish.breedingNotes: The Juruá Dwarf Cichlid is a cave-spawner in which the female provides intensive brood care. Breeding requires very soft, acidic blackwater (pH 4.5–5.5, TDS below 80 µS), and conditioning breeders with live and frozen foods significantly improves spawning success. Males are haremic. Clutch size ranges from 40 to 100 eggs, and fry typically become free-swimming around 10–14 days after hatching, at which point they accept baby brine shrimp (Artemia nauplii).
